access.guest_fallback_message: Detailed explanations are available for logged-in Saola students only.

q14.explanation: Câu 14 khớp với thông tin ở đoạn 1. Ý chính trong bài là: "Where the Pregolya river meets the Baltic sea, the Russian enclave of Kaliningrad looks out across the Vistula lagoon towards Gdansk in Poland. In past times these two cities formed part of a vast network of trading cities stretching from Estonia to England, flourishing through the later Medieval period thanks to a commercial and defensive confederation of merchant guilds. Reaching the height of its prosperity in the late sixteenth century, thanks to its export of wheat, timber, hemp and furs, Kaliningrad was originally called Koenigsberg and was visited by more than one hundred ships annually.". Vì câu hỏi diễn đạt lại cùng một ý, đáp án là TRUE.
q14.evidence: Where the Pregolya river meets the Baltic sea, the Russian enclave of Kaliningrad looks out across the Vistula lagoon towards Gdansk in Poland. In past times these two cities formed part of a vast network of trading cities stretching from Estonia to England, flourishing through the later Medieval period thanks to a commercial and defensive confederation of merchant guilds. Reaching the height of its prosperity in the late sixteenth century, thanks to its export of wheat, timber, hemp and furs, Kaliningrad was originally called Koenigsberg and was visited by more than one hundred ships annually._

q15.explanation: Câu 15 có một số từ khóa gần với đoạn 1, nhưng bài không cung cấp đủ chi tiết để xác nhận hay phủ định toàn bộ mệnh đề. Câu gần nhất là: "In past times these two cities formed part of a vast network of trading cities stretching from Estonia to England, flourishing through the later Medieval period thanks to a commercial and defensive confederation of merchant guilds. Reaching the height of its prosperity in the late sixteenth century, thanks to its export of wheat, timber, hemp and furs, Kaliningrad was originally called Koenigsberg and was visited by more than one hundred ships annually. The resulting wealth allowed the people of the city to construct seven bridges connecting the banks of the river and two large central islands together.". Vì thiếu thông tin trực tiếp, đáp án là NOT GIVEN.
q15.evidence: In past times these two cities formed part of a vast network of trading cities stretching from Estonia to England, flourishing through the later Medieval period thanks to a commercial and defensive confederation of merchant guilds. Reaching the height of its prosperity in the late sixteenth century, thanks to its export of wheat, timber, hemp and furs, Kaliningrad was originally called Koenigsberg and was visited by more than one hundred ships annually. The resulting wealth allowed the people of the city to construct seven bridges connecting the banks of the river and two large central islands together._

q16.explanation: Câu 16 mâu thuẫn với thông tin ở đoạn 3. Bài nêu: "Euler was a tremendously industrious academic, publishing around 500 books and papers over the course of his life, so to many it seemed odd that his interest would be piqued by such a trivial logical problem. In fact, when the mayor of Gdansk originally contacted him to probe his mind for a solution to the problem in a letter of 1736, Euler’s response indicated his initial reluctance due to the problem “bearing little relationship to mathematics”, suggesting that the solution would be “based on reason alone” and that its discovery “does not depend on any mathematical principle.” However, it seems that Euler’s inclination towards certainty and understanding was too strong to resist, as a letter written soon after to a friend, Giovanni Marinoni, explains that in Euler’s view the Koenigsberg problem “...is so banal, but seemed to me worthy of attention in that neither geometry, nor algebra, nor even the art of counting was sufficient to solve it.”". Câu hỏi đã đổi một chi tiết quan trọng hoặc đảo ngược ý trong bài, nên đáp án là FALSE.
q16.evidence: Euler was a tremendously industrious academic, publishing around 500 books and papers over the course of his life, so to many it seemed odd that his interest would be piqued by such a trivial logical problem. In fact, when the mayor of Gdansk originally contacted him to probe his mind for a solution to the problem in a letter of 1736, Euler’s response indicated his initial reluctance due to the problem “bearing little relationship to mathematics”, suggesting that the solution would be “based on reason alone” and that its discovery “does not depend on any mathematical principle.” However, it seems that Euler’s inclination towards certainty and understanding was too strong to resist, as a letter written soon after to a friend, Giovanni Marinoni, explains that in Euler’s view the Koenigsberg problem “...is so banal, but seemed to me worthy of attention in that neither geometry, nor algebra, nor even the art of counting was sufficient to solve it.”_
